## [00:00 - 03:01]

I wanted to pop in here really quick to talk a bit about communication and influence. More of the idea of ethical influence. Some people think influence could be a bad thing. Think you're trying to take advantage of people to do something or manipulate them into doing something they don't want. And yes, if somebody's trying to do that or trying to sell something bad, a bad car, sell drugs, gambling to someone with an issue, with gambling, yes, that's bad. But that's not what we do here. That's not what we get to do for a living. We create products and services that empower people. We get to shorten the gap in their life. We get to take them from where they are to where they want to be, fall in love with their impact and their transformation. And with that, if we truly love what we do, and I might say this way too many times, then we are doing a disservice if we don't influence people to say yes. Because without us, they may never reach their goals or they might go to someone else that doesn't care as much as you. So instead, let's just focus on ethical influence, helping people take an action that can help them reach their goals or solve the problems that they're dealing with. It's not about tricking anybody into anything. It's about using the knowledge we have about human psychology and behavior to help people make a decision, to move forward, as I said, reach their goals, period. And one of the strongest tools we have in our ethical influence toolbox is our language. It's the art, the art of messaging, the words we use with ourselves, but the words we use with our audience and your students and your customers and your clients can determine how successful you will be. So in the next couple of videos, we've got some great teaching on communication, including, literally, I might have said this too many times too, my most favorite strategy on the planet. It has transformed my life. It's called hook story close. You will never think about sharing, selling, doing a live, doing a webcast, or whatever it is, you'll never look at it again the same way because you will have a philosophy behind it. You will have a context, you will have a framework that changes everything. You'll learn about storytelling. You'll learn about copywriting basics. Got a right copy that gets people to take action. You get to dive in and learn how to more effectively communicate and influence your audience to take an action that could change your life and grow your business. But before you go there, here's a quick Tony moment, a classic, where he talks about the importance of the words we use with ourselves. It's just a few minutes long. So if you want to watch the whole thing, we'll have it in the Tony Robbins collection, but if not right now, you can watch a quick clip and then dive right in to communication and ethical influence. So real quick, words play a huge role in what you feel.

## [03:01 - 05:22]

See when something happens, the first thing that controls us really is the way we're feeling already. If you're in a crappy state, if your body is under stress, if you're in a lousy emotional state, but if you're in a lousy physical state, you're going to interpret things very differently than if you're feeling passionate or playful or outrageous. So I teach three things usually. I teach people how to change their physical body because if you try to change your mind, I'm happy, I'm happy, I'm happy. You know, your brain goes BS. You know, I'm not happy. You read between the lines kind of thing. But if you change your body radically enough, and if you've done any of the research or you've worked with some of the programs I have, you know, change your physiology and you'll change how you feel if you change it radically. And so that's the way you use your voice. It's the way you move. It's the way you breathe. The second one I always teach is changing your focus because whatever you focus on, you're going to feel. You know, if you focus on what's wrong, everything's wrong. What's wrong is always available if you look for it, but so is what's right. And so controlling what you focus on and how you focus is another skill. But there's a third thing that controls how you feel, and that is the meaning we give things. And as soon as you focus on something, you have to decide what does this mean? Is this the end or the beginning? See, if you think it's the end of your career, the end of a relationship versus the beginning of your career, beginning of a relationship, you're going to have a completely different set of mindsets, different emotions. And from those emotions, you're going to act very differently. If you think it's the end of a relationship, are you going to behave the same way as if you think it's the beginning? Obviously not. In fact, I often tell people, you know, when you're really in the beginning of a relationship, it's kind of like what you do when you're in the beginning of business, hopefully. Your whole focus is on how to blow away your customer, in this case, the love of your life, but you want to create a raving fan, not a satisfied customer, right? You want to do more for the minimum, and you're not measuring what you're getting or not. And so a lot of it has to do with focus and meaning, but the words we use, those words play a huge role. If you think somebody does something and then immediately you go, they're disrespecting me or they're dissing me for short, right? Or you think that they're challenging you, or you think they're coaching you, or you think they're really loving on you. One word change instantly changes how you feel, and your emotions are what drive your decisions and your actions. If you're angry and pissed off, you're obviously going to make different decisions and behave a different way than if you're feeling playful or grateful. So one of the things they noticed was we have habits of words we use, and those habits actually create our emotions.
